steps.title: Compare the two interpretations
steps.content: Text 1 reports that magpies without trackers pecked at another magpie's tracker until it detached, and the researchers suggest this could show one magpie helping another without self-benefit. Text 2 cautions that tracker removal can also come from curiosity about novel equipment, not deliberate assistance.

steps.title: Reject mismatched responses
steps.content: Choice A shifts the novelty from the tracker to the captive setting, which Text 2 does not do. Choice B invents a motive of obtaining the tracker for self-benefit. Choice C focuses on whether all captured magpies showed the behavior, but Text 2 questions the interpretation of intent, not the number of participants.

steps.title: State Text two's likely reply
steps.content: The author of Text 2 would most likely say the same behavior could be explained by curiosity about equipment. That response leaves open the observed pecking but removes the need to conclude that the magpies were trying to assist another magpie. The answer is D.
